questiontext.text: A shopkeeper marks his goods 40% above cost price and allows two successive discounts of 10% and 15%. What is his net profit or loss percentage?

incorrectfeedback.text: Incorrect. MP = 140, after 10%: 126, after 15%: 107.1. Net profit = 7.1%.

questiontext.text: A 300 m long train passes a stationary pole in 15 seconds. How long will it take to cross a bridge 450 m long?
A can do a piece of work in 18 days, B in 12 days. A starts the work and after every 3 days, they work together for 1 day. In how many days will the work be completed?
Identify the correct analogy: Barometer : Pressure :: Seismograph : ?

incorrectfeedback.text: A barometer measures pressure; a seismograph measures earthquakes.

questiontext.text: The ratio of ages of P and Q is 3:5. After 10 years, the ratio will be 5:7. What is the present age of Q?
Find the next term: 2, 5, 10, 17, 26, ?
A sum of money doubles itself at simple interest in 8 years. In how many years will it become 5 times itself?

incorrectfeedback.text: Doubles in 8 years → rate = 100/8 = 12.5% p.a. To become 5 times: interest = 4P, T = 4P/(P×12.5/100) = 32 years.

questiontext.text: The perimeter of a semicircle is 72 cm. What is its area? (Use π = 22/7)

incorrectfeedback.text: Perimeter of semicircle = πr + 2r = r(π+2) = 72. r×(36/7) = 72 → r = 14. Area = πr²/2 = (22/7)×196/2 = 308 cm².

questiontext.text: Ravi starts facing North. He turns 135° clockwise, then 90° anticlockwise, then 45° clockwise. Which direction is he now facing?
Pointing to a photograph, Meena says, "She is the daughter of my grandfather's only son." How is the person in the photograph related to Meena?

incorrectfeedback.text: Grandfather's only son = Meena's father. Father's daughter = Meena's sister.

questiontext.text: A bag contains 4 red, 5 blue, and 6 green balls. Two balls are drawn at random without replacement. What is the probability that both are of different colours?
Identify the odd one out: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Moon, Mars

incorrectfeedback.text: Moon is a natural satellite, not a planet. All others are planets in the solar system.

questiontext.text: In a certain code, STUDENT is written as VWXGHQW. How is TEACHER written in the same code?

incorrectfeedback.text: Each letter shifted by +3: T→W, E→H, A→D, C→F, H→K, E→H, R→U → WHDFKHU.

questiontext.text: The HCF of two numbers is 16 and their LCM is 480. If one number is 96, what is the other number?

incorrectfeedback.text: Product of two numbers = HCF × LCM = 16 × 480 = 7680. Other number = 7680/96 = 80.

questiontext.text:
Statements: All flowers are trees. No tree is a stone.
Conclusions: (I) No flower is a stone. (II) Some stones are flowers.

incorrectfeedback.text: All flowers are trees, no tree is a stone → no flower is a stone (I follows). Conclusion II does not follow.

questiontext.text: A function \(f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}\) is lower semicontinuous at \(x_0\) if \(\liminf_{x \to x_0} f(x) \geq f(x_0)\). Which of the following is lower semicontinuous everywhere on \(\mathbb{R}\)?

incorrectfeedback.text: f(x)=0 for x≠0, f(0)=1 is upper semicontinuous at 0 (not lower). f(x)=|x| is continuous hence both. The indicator of [0,∞) is lower semicontinuous. The floor function ⌊x⌋ is upper semicontinuous.

questiontext.text: The Vitali Convergence Theorem states that if \(\{f_n\}\) is a sequence in \(L^1(\mu)\) where \(\mu\) is finite, and \(f_n \to f\) in measure, then \(\|f_n - f\|_1 \to 0\) if and only if \(\{f_n\}\) is:

incorrectfeedback.text: The Vitali Convergence Theorem characterizes L¹ convergence via uniform integrability of the sequence.

questiontext.text: The Hahn Decomposition Theorem states that for a signed measure \(\nu\) on a measurable space \((X, \mathcal{F})\), there exist sets \(P\) and \(N\) such that:

incorrectfeedback.text: Hahn decomposition: X = P ∪ N with P positive set, N negative set for ν, and P ∩ N = ∅.

answer.text: \(X = P \cup N\), \(P \cap N = \emptyset\), \(P\) is positive and \(N\) is negative for \(\nu\)

\(
u\) can be written as \(
u = 
u^+ - 
u^-\) with both measures finite
There exists a positive measure \(\mu\) such that \(\nu \ll \mu\)

questiontext.text: The Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse \(A^+\) of a matrix \(A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}\) with SVD \(A = U\Sigma V^T\) is given by:

incorrectfeedback.text: If A = UΣVᵀ then A⁺ = VΣ⁺Uᵀ where Σ⁺ replaces each nonzero σᵢ by 1/σᵢ.

questiontext.text: The companion matrix of the polynomial \(p(\lambda) = \lambda^3 - 6\lambda^2 + 11\lambda - 6\) is:

incorrectfeedback.text: Companion matrix has last column [6, -11, 6]ᵀ and subdiagonal identity. Its characteristic polynomial equals p(λ).

questiontext.text: The group with presentation \(\langle a, b \mid a^4 = b^2 = e,\, bab^{-1} = a^{-1} \rangle\) is isomorphic to:

incorrectfeedback.text: This is the dihedral group D₄ (symmetries of square) of order 8.

questiontext.text: A Dedekind domain is an integral domain that is:

incorrectfeedback.text: Dedekind domain: Noetherian, integrally closed, every nonzero prime ideal is maximal. Examples: rings of integers in number fields.

answer.text: Noetherian, integrally closed, and a UFD
Noetherian, integrally closed, and every nonzero prime ideal is maximal
A PID with exactly one maximal ideal
A UFD in which every ideal is principal

questiontext.text: Jensen's formula relates the zeros \(a_1, \ldots, a_n\) of an analytic function \(f\) inside \(|z| = r\) to:

incorrectfeedback.text: Jensen's formula: log|f(0)| = Σlog(|aₖ|/r) + (1/2π)∫₀²π log|f(re^{iθ})|dθ.

answer.text: The maximum modulus of \(f\) on \(|z| = r\)
The mean value of \(\log|f|\) on the circle \(|z| = r\)
The residues of \(f\) at its poles

questiontext.text: Montel's theorem states that a family \(\mathcal{F}\) of holomorphic functions on a domain \(\Omega\) is normal (every sequence has a locally uniformly convergent subsequence) if and only if \(\mathcal{F}\) is:

incorrectfeedback.text: Montel's theorem: a family of holomorphic functions is normal iff it is locally uniformly bounded (equivalently locally equibounded).

questiontext.text: The Stone-Čech compactification \(\beta X\) of a completely regular Hausdorff space \(X\) is characterised by the property that every bounded continuous \(f: X \to \mathbb{R}\) extends to:

incorrectfeedback.text: Universal property of βX: every bounded continuous real-valued function on X extends uniquely to a continuous function on βX.

answer.text: A uniformly continuous function on \(X\) only

questiontext.text: Grönwall's inequality states: if \(u(t) \leq \alpha(t) + \int_0^t \beta(s)u(s)\,ds\) with \(\beta \geq 0\), then:

incorrectfeedback.text: Grönwall: u(t) ≤ α(t) + ∫β·u gives u(t) ≤ α(t)exp(∫₀ᵗ β(s)ds). Used to establish uniqueness of ODE solutions.

answer.text: \(u(t) \leq \alpha(0) e^{\beta t}\) only if \(\alpha\) is constant

questiontext.text: A Schauder basis \(\{e_n\}\) for a Banach space \(X\) is a sequence such that every \(x \in X\) can be written uniquely as \(x = \sum_{n=1}^\infty c_n e_n\). Which space has the Haar system as a Schauder basis?

incorrectfeedback.text: The Haar system forms a Schauder basis for Lᵖ[0,1] for 1 ≤ p < ∞.

answer.text: The space of bounded variation functions \(BV[0,1]\)

questiontext.text: The Eberlein-Šmulian theorem states that in a Banach space, a set is relatively weakly compact if and only if it is:

incorrectfeedback.text: Eberlein-Šmulian: In a Banach space, relative weak compactness ⟺ relative weak sequential compactness (every sequence has a weakly convergent subsequence).

answer.text: Equicontinuous as a family of functionals

questiontext.text: If \(\sqrt{n}(X_n - \mu) \xrightarrow{d} N(0, \sigma^2)\) and \(g\) is differentiable with \(g'(\mu) \neq 0\), then by the Delta Method:

incorrectfeedback.text: Delta method: √n(g(Xₙ)−g(μ)) →ᵈ N(0, [g'(μ)]²σ²).

questiontext.text: The Wilcoxon signed-rank test is a nonparametric alternative to the:]]>

incorrectfeedback.text: Wilcoxon signed-rank test is used instead of the paired t-test when normality cannot be assumed.

questiontext.text: For the system \(Ax = b\), the Jacobi iterative method converges if:

incorrectfeedback.text: Jacobi iteration converges if A is strictly diagonally dominant: |aᵢᵢ| > Σⱼ≠ᵢ|aᵢⱼ| for all i.

questiontext.text: The central difference approximation for \(f''(x)\) with step size \(h\) is:

incorrectfeedback.text: Central difference: f''(x) ≈ [f(x+h) − 2f(x) + f(x−h)]/h² with error O(h²).

questiontext.text: The Ramsey number \(R(3,3)\) equals:

incorrectfeedback.text: R(3,3)=6: any 2-colouring of edges of K₆ contains a monochromatic K₃. K₅ can be 2-coloured with no monochromatic triangle.

questiontext.text: The Möbius inversion formula states: if \(g(n) = \sum_{d \mid n} f(d)\), then:

incorrectfeedback.text: Möbius inversion: g = f * 1 implies f = g * μ, so f(n) = Σ_{d|n} μ(d)g(n/d).

questiontext.text: A topological space is paracompact if every open cover has a locally finite open refinement. Which of the following is always paracompact?

incorrectfeedback.text: Every metric space is paracompact (Stone's theorem). Compact spaces are paracompact. Not all Hausdorff spaces are paracompact.

questiontext.text: A fiber bundle \(F \hookrightarrow E \xrightarrow{p} B\) with base \(B\), total space \(E\), and fiber \(F\) gives a long exact sequence of homotopy groups. What is the connecting map \(\partial: \pi_n(B) \to \pi_{n-1}(F)\)?

incorrectfeedback.text: The long exact sequence of a fibration: … → πₙ(F) → πₙ(E) → πₙ(B) →∂ πₙ₋₁(F) → … arises from the homotopy lifting property.

answer.text: It is always the zero map
It arises from the homotopy lifting property of the fibration
It equals the induced map on homology groups
It is defined only when \(B\) is simply connected

questiontext.text: Compared to Jacobi iteration, the Gauss-Seidel method for solving \(Ax = b\):

incorrectfeedback.text: Gauss-Seidel uses updated values immediately. It generally converges faster than Jacobi and uses only one storage array. For symmetric positive definite A, Gauss-Seidel converges.

answer.text: Requires more storage and is generally slower
Uses updated component values immediately and generally converges faster
Always converges when Jacobi converges but not vice versa
Requires the matrix to be positive definite, unlike Jacobi

questiontext.text: Pólya's enumeration theorem counts distinct colorings of an object under a symmetry group \(G\) acting on positions. The number of distinct colorings using \(m\) colors is given by the cycle index \(Z(G)\) evaluated at:

incorrectfeedback.text: Pólya: number of colorings = Z(G; m, m, …, m) = Z(G) with each sₖ replaced by m.

questiontext.text: In a one-way ANOVA with \(k\) groups and total \(N\) observations, the F-statistic is the ratio of:

incorrectfeedback.text: F = MSB/MSW = [SSB/(k−1)] / [SSW/(N−k)]. Degrees of freedom: between k−1, within N−k.

answer.text: Total sum of squares to error sum of squares
Mean square between groups \(\text{MSB} = \text{SSB}/(k-1)\) to mean square within groups \(\text{MSW} = \text{SSW}/(N-k)\)
Sum of squares between to degrees of freedom \(N-1\)

questiontext.text: Simpson's 1/3 rule for numerical integration over \([a, b]\) with midpoint \(m = (a+b)/2\) is:

incorrectfeedback.text: Simpson's 1/3 rule: ∫ₐᵇf dx ≈ (b−a)/6 × [f(a) + 4f(m) + f(b)]. Exact for polynomials of degree ≤ 3.

questiontext.text: [MSQ] A family \(\{f_\alpha\}\) in \(L^1(\mu)\) is uniformly integrable if and only if which of the following hold? (Select all that apply)

incorrectfeedback.text: UI equivalent conditions: sup∫|fα|<∞ (bounded in L¹), for every ε>0 there exists δ>0 such that μ(E)<δ → sup∫_E|fα|<ε, and the family is tight (for every ε>0 there exists compact K with sup∫_{K^c}|fα|<ε in finite measure space).

answer.text: For every \(\varepsilon > 0\) there exists \(\delta > 0\) such that \(\mu(E) < \delta \Rightarrow \sup_\alpha \int_E |f_\alpha|\,d\mu < \varepsilon\)
For every \(\varepsilon > 0\) there exists \(M > 0\) such that \(\sup_\alpha \int_{\{|f_\alpha| > M\}} |f_\alpha|\,d\mu < \varepsilon\)
Every subsequence has a pointwise convergent sub-subsequence

questiontext.text: [MSQ] Which of the following statements about reflexive Banach spaces are TRUE?

incorrectfeedback.text: Reflexive: X ≅ X** via canonical map. Every bounded sequence has a weakly convergent subsequence (Eberlein-Šmulian). Lᵖ is reflexive for 1<p<∞. L¹ and L∞ are NOT reflexive.

answer.text: A Banach space \(X\) is reflexive iff the canonical map \(J: X \to X^{**}\) is surjective
In a reflexive Banach space, every bounded sequence has a weakly convergent subsequence
A closed subspace of a reflexive space is reflexive

questiontext.text: [MSQ] Which of the following statements about projective and injective modules are TRUE?

incorrectfeedback.text: Projective: direct summand of free module, every epi splits. Injective: Baer's criterion. Over a field, every module is both projective and injective (free = projective = injective for vector spaces).

answer.text: A module \(P\) is projective iff every short exact sequence \(0 \to A \to B \to P \to 0\) splits
Over a field, every module (vector space) is both projective and injective
A module \(Q\) is injective iff it is a direct summand of every module that contains it

questiontext.text: [MSQ] Which of the following statements about Riemann surfaces are TRUE?

incorrectfeedback.text: A Riemann surface is a connected 1-dimensional complex manifold. The Riemann sphere ℂ∪{∞} is compact. The uniformization theorem: every simply connected Riemann surface is conformally equivalent to ℂ, the disk, or ℂ∪{∞}.

answer.text: Every Riemann surface is a 2-dimensional real manifold
The Riemann sphere \(\mathbb{C} \cup \{\infty\}\) is a compact Riemann surface of genus 0
By the Uniformization Theorem, every simply connected Riemann surface is conformally equivalent to \(\mathbb{C}\), \(\mathbb{D}\), or \(\mathbb{P}^1\)
Every Riemann surface admits a holomorphic function with no zeros

questiontext.text: [MSQ] Which of the following comparison/maximum principles are correctly stated?

incorrectfeedback.text: Weak maximum principle for harmonic functions: max on closure equals max on boundary. Parabolic max principle: max over domain is achieved on the parabolic boundary. Comparison principle for ODE: if u'≤f(t,u) and v'≥f(t,v) with u(0)≤v(0) then u≤v.

answer.text: For a harmonic function on a bounded domain, the maximum is attained on the boundary
For the heat equation \(u_t = \Delta u\), the maximum of \(u\) over \(\Omega_T = \Omega \times (0,T)\) is attained on the parabolic boundary
If \(Lu \leq 0 \leq Lv\) for an elliptic operator \(L\) and \(u \geq v\) on \(\partial\Omega\), then \(u \geq v\) in \(\Omega\)
Grönwall's inequality provides a quantitative comparison for integral inequalities involving ODE solutions

questiontext.text: [MSQ] Which of the following statements about valuation rings are TRUE?

incorrectfeedback.text: Valuation ring: for every x in fraction field K, either x∈V or x⁻¹∈V. Every valuation ring is a local ring. Discrete valuation rings (DVRs) are PIDs. ℤ_(p) = {a/b: p∤b} is a DVR.

answer.text: A valuation ring \(V\) in a field \(K\) satisfies: for every \(x \in K\setminus\{0\}\), either \(x \in V\) or \(x^{-1} \in V\)
Every valuation ring is a local ring
A discrete valuation ring (DVR) is a principal ideal domain with exactly one nonzero prime ideal
The ring \(\mathbb{Z}_{(p)} = \{a/b \in \mathbb{Q} : p \nmid b\}\) is a DVR with uniformizer \(p\)

questiontext.text: [MSQ] Let \(\mathbb{F}_n\) be the empirical CDF of i.i.d. samples from \(F\). Which of the following are TRUE?

incorrectfeedback.text: Glivenko-Cantelli: sup|Fₙ−F|→0 a.s. DKW inequality: P(sup|Fₙ−F|>ε)≤2e^{−2nε²}. The empirical process √n(Fₙ−F) converges weakly to a Brownian bridge.

answer.text: The empirical process \(\sqrt{n}(\mathbb{F}_n - F)\) converges weakly to a Brownian bridge
The empirical CDF \(\mathbb{F}_n\) is always differentiable

questiontext.text: [MSQ] In the bootstrap method for statistical inference, which of the following are TRUE?

incorrectfeedback.text: Bootstrap: resample with replacement from original sample. Consistent for smooth statistics. Bootstrap CI methods include percentile, BCa. Parametric bootstrap samples from a fitted parametric model.

answer.text: The nonparametric bootstrap draws samples with replacement from the observed data
The bootstrap is consistent for estimating the distribution of smooth functionals of the empirical CDF
Bootstrap confidence intervals can be constructed using the percentile method or the bias-corrected and accelerated (BCa) method
The parametric bootstrap simulates data from the parametric model fitted to the observed data

questiontext.text: [MSQ] The Urysohn Metrization Theorem and related results: which of the following are TRUE?

incorrectfeedback.text: Urysohn metrization: every second countable regular (T₃) space is metrizable. A compact Hausdorff space is normal. Nagata-Smirnov: metrizable iff T₁, regular, and has a σ-locally finite base.

answer.text: Every second countable regular (T\(_3\)) Hausdorff space is metrizable
Every compact Hausdorff space is normal (T\(_4\))
A space is metrizable iff it is \(T_1\), regular, and has a \(\sigma\)-locally finite base (Nagata-Smirnov theorem)

questiontext.text: [MSQ] Schauder estimates for elliptic PDE \(Lu = f\) in Hölder spaces: which of the following are TRUE?

incorrectfeedback.text: Interior Schauder estimates: ‖u‖_{C^{2,α}(Ω')} ≤ C(‖u‖_{C⁰(Ω)}+‖f‖_{C^{0,α}(Ω)}). Global estimates require C^{2,α} boundary. Solutions are as smooth as data allows (elliptic regularity).

answer.text: Interior Schauder estimates bound \(\|u\|_{C^{2,\alpha}(\Omega')}\) in terms of \(\|u\|_{C^0(\Omega)}\) and \(\|f\|_{C^{0,\alpha}(\Omega)}\) for \(\Omega' \Subset \Omega\)
Global Schauder estimates require the boundary \(\partial\Omega\) to be of class \(C^{2,\alpha}\)
Elliptic regularity: if \(f \in C^{k,\alpha}\) and \(L\) has \(C^{k,\alpha}\) coefficients, then \(u \in C^{k+2,\alpha}\)
Schauder estimates hold for all \(L^p\) spaces without any regularity on coefficients

questiontext.text: [MSQ] For a CW complex \(X\), which of the following properties of cellular homology are TRUE?

incorrectfeedback.text: Cellular = singular homology on CW complexes. Chain group C_n generated by n-cells. Boundary maps computable via degree theory. Euler characteristic = alternating sum of Betti numbers = alternating sum of cell counts.

answer.text: Cellular homology of a CW complex equals its singular homology
The cellular chain group \(C_n(X)\) is free abelian with one generator for each \(n\)-cell
The cellular boundary map \(\partial_n: C_n \to C_{n-1}\) is computed using the degrees of attaching maps

questiontext.text: [MSQ] Let \(\mathcal{S}^n_+\) denote the cone of \(n \times n\) positive semidefinite matrices. Which of the following are TRUE?

incorrectfeedback.text: PSD cone is convex, closed. Its dual cone is itself (self-dual). Sum of PSD matrices is PSD. The set of PD matrices is an open subset of symmetric matrices. Rank-1 PSD matrices are extreme rays.

answer.text: The set \(\mathcal{S}^n_+\) is a closed convex cone in the space of symmetric matrices
The cone \(\mathcal{S}^n_+\) is self-dual: its dual cone equals itself under the trace inner product
The sum of two positive semidefinite matrices is positive semidefinite
The set of positive definite matrices is an open dense subset of \(\mathcal{S}^n_+\)

questiontext.text: [MSQ] The amalgamated free product \(G = A *_C B\) (where \(C\) is a common subgroup of \(A\) and \(B\)) satisfies which of the following?

incorrectfeedback.text: Amalgamated product is the pushout in the category of groups. When C={e}, it is the free product A*B. Seifert-van Kampen: π₁(X∪Y) = π₁(X)*_{π₁(X∩Y)}π₁(Y). Elements have normal forms as alternating products from A\C and B\C.

answer.text: When \(C = \{e\}\), the amalgamated product \(A *_C B\) is the free product \(A * B\)
The Seifert-van Kampen theorem expresses \(\pi_1(X \cup Y)\) as an amalgamated free product when \(X \cap Y\) is path-connected
Every element of \(A *_C B\) has a unique normal form as an alternating product of elements from \(A \setminus C\) and \(B \setminus C\)
The amalgamated free product \(A *_C B\) is always a finite group when \(A\) and \(B\) are finite

questiontext.text: [MSQ] The weak formulation of the Dirichlet problem \(-\Delta u = f\) in \(\Omega\), \(u = 0\) on \(\partial\Omega\): which of the following are TRUE?

incorrectfeedback.text: Weak form: find u∈H₀¹ such that ∫∇u·∇v = ∫fv for all v∈H₀¹. Lax-Milgram gives unique existence when bilinear form is coercive and bounded. By elliptic regularity: if f∈L², then u∈H². Test functions are compactly supported smooth functions.

answer.text: The weak solution \(u \in H_0^1(\Omega)\) satisfies \(\int_\Omega \nabla u \cdot \nabla v\,dx = \int_\Omega f v\,dx\) for all \(v \in H_0^1(\Omega)\)
Existence and uniqueness follow from the Lax-Milgram theorem applied to the coercive bilinear form \(a(u,v) = \int \nabla u \cdot \nabla v\)
If \(f \in L^2(\Omega)\), elliptic regularity gives \(u \in H^2(\Omega)\) when \(\partial\Omega\) is smooth
Smooth compactly supported functions \(C_c^\infty(\Omega)\) serve as valid test functions in the weak formulation

questiontext.text: [MSQ] Let \(\phi(n)\) denote Euler's totient. Which of the following are TRUE?

incorrectfeedback.text: φ(n) = n∏(1−1/p) over primes p|n. Σ_{d|n}φ(d)=n (inclusion-exclusion). φ is multiplicative. φ(p^k)=p^k−p^{k−1}.

answer.text: \(\phi(n) = n \prod_{p \mid n} \left(1 - \frac{1}{p}\right)\) where the product is over distinct prime divisors of \(n\)
\(\sum_{d \mid n} \phi(d) = n\) for all positive integers \(n\)
\(\phi(p^k) = p^k - p^{k-1}\) for any prime \(p\) and \(k \geq 1\)
